title: Trends: The Long-Term Direction
concept: A trend is the long-term upward or downward movement in a time series, representing the general direction the data is moving over a long period.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is temporal dependency in time series?

options.text: The fact that future values in the sequence depend on past values.
The need to scale all time columns to Z-scores.

exercises.explain: Correct! Temporal dependency means sequence order matters because the past influences the future.

cards.title: The growing town
cards.body: If you track a town's water consumption daily, it bounces up and down based on weather. But if you zoom out over 10 years, you see a steady upward slope. The town is growing, so water demand is rising. This long-term drift is what we call a trend.

cards.title: Trend: The long-term slope
cards.body: A trend is a persistent, long-term increase or decrease in the baseline value of a time series. It represents the underlying movement of the data once you smooth out short-term fluctuations and noise.

cards.title: A ship drifting on a current
cards.body: Imagine a ship sailing. Due to waves, it rocks left and right every few seconds. But a slow, steady ocean current is carrying the ship miles to the east over several hours. The waves are short-term noise; the steady drift is the trend.

cards.title: Types of trends
cards.body: 1. **Upward Trend**: Global internet usage over the last 30 years (steady rise), 2. **Downward Trend**: Mail postage volume over the last 20 years (steady decline), 3. **Horizontal/Flat Trend**: Stable daily body temperature of a healthy person (no drift).

cards.title: Trends don't continue forever
cards.body: It's easy to assume an upward trend will go on indefinitely. In business forecasting, projecting a linear trend too far into the future (extrapolation) is a common trap. Trends can slow down, plateau, or reverse due to market limits or events.

cards.title: Trend Directions
cards.body: The general movement of a timeline over months or years:

left.text:
Data values rise on average.
e.g. population growth.
Positive slope.

right.text:
Data values fall on average.
e.g. analog film sales.
Negative slope.

exercises.prompt: What does a trend represent in a time series?

options.text: The long-term upward or downward direction the data moves over time.
Short-term repeating cycles that happen every week.
Random noise that cannot be explained.

exercises.explain: Correct! Trend is the long-term underlying direction of the data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An upward trend in sales means sales must increase every single consecutive day without exception.
exercises.explain: False! Daily sales can fluctuate up and down due to weekends or noise, but the trend describes the overall long-term direction.

exercises.sentence: Projecting a current trend far into the future is called ___ and can be highly risky.
exercises.explain: Extrapolation is predicting values beyond the range of the training data.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is an example of a downward trend?

options.text: A smartphone manufacturer notices their landline phone sales have steadily dropped year-over-year for a decade.
A retail store notices sales spike every December during the holidays.
A server's memory usage spikes at midnight then drops back to normal by 1:00 AM.

exercises.explain: Correct! A steady decline over a decade is a downward trend.

exercises.prompt: A streaming platform notices that their subscriber count has increased by 10% every year for 5 years (Upward Trend). However, during the summer months, sign-ups consistently slow down before picking up in winter. How should we analyze this behavior?

options.text: The subscriber count has a long-term upward trend, combined with short-term seasonal fluctuations.
The trend has reversed and is now downward because of the summer dip.
The data is completely random noise and cannot be modeled.
The upward trend has caused the summer drop.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The overall drift is upward (trend), but it is influenced by repeating seasonal shifts (summer drops). Next, let's learn how we identify these repeating seasonal cycles!
